the gap how it works what they found does it hold up? takeaways
2019 · EMNLP · Benchmarks & Evals

PubMedQA: A Dataset for Biomedical Research Question Answering

1,000 expert-annotated yes/no/maybe questions turn PubMed abstracts into a scientific-reasoning benchmark — the best model reaches 68.1% accuracy against a 78.0% single-human ceiling.

Qiao Jin, Bhuwan Dhingra, Zhengping Liu, William W. Cohen, Xinghua Lu
venue EMNLP citations 1,770 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Question answering is the standard way to test whether a model reasons over text rather than pattern-matches keywords, but by 2019 the large, high-quality QA datasets — SQuAD, HotpotQA, Natural Questions — were all general-domain, drawn from Wikipedia or web search logs. Biomedical QA had the opposite problem: the largest expert-annotated set, BioASQ, had fewer than 3,000 training instances, and most were simple factoid lookups. Datasets built automatically at scale (emrQA, BioRead) got size but lost hardness — their answers can usually be extracted from the passage as a contiguous span with little inference required. Meanwhile, PubMed abstracts already contain, in miniature, exactly the kind of reasoning a scientific-QA benchmark would want to test: nearly every clinical research article states a question in its title, reports quantitative results in its body, and then commits to a conclusion. The authors found that more than half of PubMed's roughly 760k question-titled articles can be answered with a bare yes/no/maybe — versus just 1% of Natural Questions and 6% of HotpotQA — and that the intervening step, deciding whether a reported statistic supports or refutes the title's claim, is a genuine act of quantitative inference rather than span extraction. No existing dataset isolated that specific skill at scale.

// how it works

The method

PubMedQA repurposes structure PubMed authors already impose on themselves. Starting from roughly 760k PubMed articles whose titles are phrased as questions, the authors kept the ~120k with a structured abstract (subsections like Objective/Methods/Results/Conclusion). Each such article yields one QA instance for free: the question is the title, the context is the abstract with its conclusive part stripped out, the long answer is that withheld conclusion, and a yes/no/maybe answer summarizes what the conclusion says (Figure 1). Because the question and the withheld answer were written by the same authors describing the same experiment, the context is guaranteed to actually address the question — a property crowd-sourced QA datasets, where a worker writes a question for an existing passage, can't guarantee.

From this pool the authors built three subsets of increasing size and decreasing supervision quality (Figure 2). PQA-L(abeled) — 1,000 instances — is expert-annotated: two annotators, both qualified M.D. candidates, independently label each instance yes/no/maybe. Annotator 1 sees the long answer (a "reasoning-free" label, since the conclusion usually states the answer outright); annotator 2 sees only the question and context and must actually infer the answer — the "reasoning-required" setting that becomes the benchmark's real test condition. Disagreements are resolved by discussion, and instances where the two still can't agree are dropped. Of the resulting 1k instances, 500 form the held-out test set and 500 support 10-fold cross-validation. PQA-U(nlabeled) — 61,200 instances — is the rest of the yes/no/maybe-answerable pool with no manual label, usable for semi-supervised bootstrapping. PQA-A(rtificial) — 211,300 instances — has no human annotation at all: PubMed articles whose titles are declarative statements (not questions) with a simple noun-phrase + verb part-of-speech pattern are converted into questions by front-shifting a copula or auxiliary verb, and the yes/no label is assigned purely from whether that verb was negated. It's a pure surface heuristic, useful for pre-training at scale but with a heavily skewed label mix (92.8% yes vs. 7.2% no, no "maybe" at all).

The paper's own baseline fine-tunes BioBERT (BERT further pre-trained on PubMed abstracts and PMC articles) in three phases rather than on PQA-L alone, since 500 training examples per fold is too little to fit a transformer well (Figure 3). Phase I fine-tunes BioBERT directly on PQA-A (question + context → label). Phase II first fine-tunes a separate BioBERT copy on PQA-A and then on PQA-L, both under the reasoning-free setting (question + long answer as input, since long answers are cheap and near-deterministic to label), to get a confident pseudo-labeler; that pseudo-labeler labels a class-balanced subset of PQA-U, and a second BioBERT copy is fine-tuned on those pseudo-labels under the real question + context setting. The Final Phase takes the Phase-I model, continues fine-tuning through Phase II's pseudo-labeled PQA-U, then finally fine-tunes on real PQA-L labels — so every subset contributes supervision despite only PQA-L being trustworthy. On top of the classification loss, an auxiliary task predicts the binary bag-of-words identity of the long answer's vocabulary from the same [CLS] embedding:

L = LQA + β·LBoW // L_BoW = −(1/N)·Σᵢ [bᵢ·log(b̂ᵢ) + (1−bᵢ)·log(1−b̂ᵢ)] — bᵢ ∈ {0,1} is whether vocabulary token i appears in the long answer; available at training time only
// what they found

The results

The headline gap is the paper's real finding. Majority-class guessing ("always yes") scores 55.20% accuracy / 23.71% macro-F1 on the PQA-L test set; the full multi-phase BioBERT with additional supervision reaches 68.08% accuracy / 52.72% macro-F1 — a real improvement over every ablation (single-phase training, dropping Phase I, dropping Phase II, and dropping the auxiliary task all score lower) — but a single human annotator working under the same reasoning-required condition (question + context only, no long answer) scores 78.00% accuracy / 72.19% macro-F1. That roughly 10-point accuracy gap, and a much wider macro-F1 gap since majority-guessing gets no credit on the minority "no" and "maybe" classes, is explicitly framed by the authors as the benchmark's headroom. Model ranking is consistent across every training schedule — BioBERT > ESIM w/ BioELMo > BiLSTM > shallow TF-IDF features > majority — and pre-training on PQA-A (Phase I) delivers the single largest jump for most models, with several hitting their best score right after Phase I, before ever seeing a real PQA-L label.

The "reasoning-free" condition (annotator sees the long answer; model gets question + long answer instead of question + context) is dramatically easier for everyone: BioBERT alone reaches 80.80% accuracy under this setting on PQA-L versus 68.08% under reasoning-required, and human performance jumps from 78.00% to 90.40%. That gap is the clearest evidence the reasoning-required task really is harder than paraphrase-matching a conclusion sentence — but it also means a share of a system's apparent "reasoning" skill could come from partially learning what the withheld conclusion would say. A sample of 200 PQA-L instances shows what the reasoning actually looks like in practice (Figure 4): 57.5% require comparing two groups' statistics against each other (e.g., a treatment vs. control rate), 16.5% require reading a subgroup breakdown, and 16.0% require reading a single group's aggregate statistic; quantitative content is involved in 96.5% of instances, and 75.5% of contexts state the interpretation of their own numbers in prose (e.g., "significantly lower") while 21.0% present bare numbers the model must interpret unaided.

Model / training scheduleAccuracy (%)Macro-F1 (%)
Majority ("always yes")55.2023.71
Shallow features, multi-phase + A.S.55.0640.67
BiLSTM, multi-phase + A.S.58.8641.06
ESIM w/ BioELMo, multi-phase + A.S.63.7247.90
BioBERT, multi-phase + A.S. (best model)68.0852.72
Human (single annotator, reasoning-required)78.0072.19
// does it hold up?

The eval read

PubMedQA's own framing — reasoning-free vs. reasoning-required — is also the sharpest lens for critiquing it. Human reasoning-free accuracy (90.40%) is 12.4 points above human reasoning-required accuracy (78.00%): most of the measured difficulty is concentrated in reconstructing the conclusion's polarity from surrounding evidence, not in some deeper multi-step inference chain. But "reconstructing the conclusion's polarity" is itself frequently closer to a paraphrase/entailment problem than to genuine quantitative reasoning: since 75.5% of contexts already contain an explicit textual verdict on their own numbers ("significantly lower," "no significant difference"), a model can often get the label right by detecting which polarity words co-occur near the compared groups, without doing arithmetic on "16% versus 33%" at all. The 21.0% of instances with bare, uninterpreted numbers are the only ones that unambiguously force real quantitative reasoning, and the paper doesn't report accuracy broken out on that harder subset — so it's not possible to tell how much of BioBERT's 68.08% comes from genuine numeric inference versus lexical polarity-cue detection.

The training recipe compounds this risk. PQA-A's 211.3k pre-training instances are labeled by a rule — was the title's verb negated? — that has nothing to do with reading an abstract, yet Phase I produces the single biggest jump in the paper's own ablations, consistent with Phase I mostly teaching a negation/keyword-polarity detector that then transfers loosely to PQA-L rather than teaching quantitative reasoning per se. There is also a structural leakage risk baked into the data-generation recipe itself: because the question is the paper's own title and the context is that same paper's own abstract, an author who titled their work as a confidently framed question has often already signaled the likely answer through word choice, independent of the numbers that follow — a known property of scientific writing (titles rarely oversell a null result) that the dataset doesn't control for.

Two more limits temper confidence in the reported gap. First, "human performance" is a single non-ensembled annotator, and the paper itself flags this as a lower bound, citing Kwiatkowski et al.'s finding that ensembled annotators score meaningfully higher — so the true human ceiling, and thus the true human-model gap, could be wider than 78.0% vs. 68.1% suggests. Second, the annotation protocol treats the two annotators' post-discussion agreed label as ground truth, but the paper's own error estimate (roughly 1%, "roughly half the product of two annotator error rates") is a back-of-envelope figure rather than a measured statistic — there's no reported pre-adjudication agreement rate (e.g., Cohen's κ), only the label after disagreements were talked through.

baselines

Five points on a real capability ladder — majority, TF-IDF shallow features, BiLSTM+word2vec, ESIM+BioELMo, BioBERT — rank consistently across every training schedule, so the headline result isn't an artifact of one architecture. But all of them are evaluated only in the reasoning-required setting on the same 500-instance test split drawn from just 1k expert labels, with no external replication set and no general-domain (non-biomedical-pretrained) BERT reported as a domain-adaptation control.

contamination

No public leaderboard existed yet in 2019, so classic train/test leakage isn't the concern. The real risk is structural: question (title) and context (abstract) were authored by the same people about the same experiment, so the question's own framing can correlate with the answer's polarity independent of the numeric evidence — a title-leakage effect the paper never measures or controls for.

what's missed

Aggregate accuracy/macro-F1 hides exactly the split that matters most: performance on the 21.0% of instances with no textual interpretation of their numbers (real numeric reasoning) versus the 75.5% where the context already states the verdict in prose (closer to paraphrase detection). The paper reports that breakdown only as a corpus statistic, never as a stratified accuracy result.

would raise confidence

An accuracy breakdown by the reasoning-type / number-interpretation strata the paper already computes; an ensembled (not single-annotator) human baseline, per the paper's own stated caveat; a measured pre-adjudication inter-annotator agreement rate; and an ablation that strips explicit-verdict language from contexts to see how much accuracy survives on bare numbers alone.

// bottom line

PubMedQA is a genuinely useful scale-up of expert-labeled biomedical QA, and its reasoning-free/reasoning-required split is an unusually honest piece of benchmark design — the paper measures its own shortcut with the same protocol it uses to measure the real task. But the roughly 10-point human-model gap it reports should be read as a mix of a harder skill (real numeric inference) and an unknown amount of an easier one (conclusion-polarity paraphrase), and until results are stratified by the number-interpretation split the paper itself provides, it isn't possible to say how much of the remaining headroom is "needs better reasoning" versus "needs better keyword detection."

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. PubMedQA turns PubMed's own question-titled, structured-abstract articles into three subsets of decreasing supervision quality — 1,000 expert-labeled (PQA-L), 61,200 unlabeled (PQA-U), 211,300 rule-labeled (PQA-A) — so a semi-supervised biomedical yes/no/maybe benchmark can exist despite expert annotation being scarce.
  2. The paper's own reasoning-free vs. reasoning-required split (human accuracy 90.40% vs. 78.00%) is the benchmark's most important number: it quantifies how much of the task can be solved by paraphrasing a withheld conclusion versus actually inferring it from the evidence.
  3. The best model — multi-phase BioBERT with long-answer bag-of-words supervision — reaches 68.08% accuracy against a 78.00% single-human ceiling and a 55.20% majority baseline: real progress over every ablation, but still a wide, unresolved gap that later medical-QA benchmarks (MultiMedQA/Med-PaLM, HealthBench) build on and try to close.
